name: OpenZerg
tagline: We don't send one smart attacker. We send a hundred dumb ones, watch which find cracks, and breed the next generation from the survivors.

mission.one_liner: Evolutionary, agentic red-team swarm that attacks a known web endpoint until it finds an exploit or converges, then explains how it got there.
mission.long: OpenZerg is a Go control plane that spawns a Kubernetes-backed swarm of PI agent pods (model: Gemma 4 via OpenRouter). Each pod is a tiny autonomous penetration tester aimed at a fixed HTTP/ws/tcp target. The control plane scores every probe by fitness, kills the weak, mutates the strong, and respawns the next generation. The loop runs until a full breach is found or max generations is reached, then writes a short human-readable summary describing the path the swarm took to its Outcome.
what_changed_from_v1: Target is now an external HTTP web app (OWASP Juice Shop, white-hat), not an in-cluster AI sandbox.
Language is Go, not Python. The Python controller/attack scripts are reference material only and will be deleted in M6.
Kubernetes cluster is the live DigitalOcean cluster (kubeconfig already configured), not Kind.
Attacker = PI agent (https://pi.dev/) running Gemma 4 via OpenRouter. Nimble.ai is used by the agent to crawl and proxy requests against the target.
Datadog integration is DROPPED for v1 to keep the 1-day scope tight. PI + Nimble satisfy the >=2 sponsor tools criterion.
Control plane is a local binary that talks to the remote cluster via the user's kubeconfig (NOT an in-cluster deployment).

hackathon.name: Agentic Engineering Hack
judging_criteria: Autonomy: agent acts on the web with real-time data, no manual intervention.
Tool Use: at least 2 sponsor tools integrated.
hackathon.scope: White-hat only. Target is a known-vulnerable demo app the team is authorized to attack. NO attempts at sandbox escape, container escape, host escape, or cloud account escalation. NO scanning of unrelated targets.

target.name: OWASP Juice Shop
known_vector_families: Reflected and stored XSS via search, feedback, comment fields
Improper input validation on /rest/products/search and admin endpoints
rules_of_engagement: Rate limit: max 10 req/s per attacker pod, max 60 req/s aggregate.
No destructive payloads (no DROP TABLE, no admin password resets that lock real users out).
Read-only exploit verification: prove the bug, don't trash the data.
Mark every request with header X-OpenZerg-Probe: true so Juice Shop ops (us) can identify our traffic.

kubernetes.existing_resources_policy: FREE_TO_DELETE. The cluster may already contain pods, deployments, services, secrets, configmaps, namespaces, or other resources from prior experiments. All of them are disposable; the ralph is authorized to `kubectl delete` anything it finds in order to make room for the openzerg namespace and its workloads. Do not preserve prior state. Exception: do NOT delete the kube-system namespace or any DigitalOcean-managed system components (do-*, csi-*, cilium*, konnectivity-*). If unsure, leave a node alone.

secrets.policy: Never commit a key. Always read from env or local file outside the repo.

openrouter_api_key.if_missing_action: Print a clear error telling the user where to put the key. Do NOT block ralph progress on code that does not need the key (compilation, unit tests, dry-run mode).

nimble_api_key.if_missing_action: Same as OpenRouter: log clearly, gate only the live-call paths.

loading_helper.behavior: Read .env file at module root (no panic if absent), then overlay process env, then return a typed Config struct. Used by both binaries.

non_goals: Do NOT attempt to attack anything other than the configured TARGET_URL.
Do NOT attempt to escape the Kubernetes pods themselves; the pods are sandboxes for our attackers, not the target.
Do NOT implement a frontend / WebSocket / React UI. v1 is CLI + structured logs only. The HTML files in repo root are v1 artifacts and will be removed in M6.
Do NOT integrate Datadog in v1. Stretch only.
Do NOT port the 18 Python attack vectors literally. The new attack vectors are HTTP-shaped (SQLi, XSS, BOLA, etc.) and the PI agent generates them with Nimble's help.
Do NOT publish the control-plane image; it runs locally.
Do NOT use Ollama, Kind, FastAPI, SQLite, or httpx (Python-era choices).
Do NOT use co-author / 'Generated with Claude' / 'Co-authored-by' trailers in commits.
principles: Every milestone must end with `go build ./...` green and (if any tests exist) `go test ./...` green.
Each PI attacker pod is disposable: spawns, attacks, prints a JSON result, exits.
The control plane is the only stateful piece. It is restartable; resumed runs are NOT required for v1.
Concurrency: 15 pods per generation, all running in parallel via k8s. Control plane uses goroutines + errgroup to read their logs.
Fail loud, fail fast. A pod that errors out scores 0.0 and the run continues.
All long-running work is cancellable via context.Context.

key_libs: net/http (Nimble + OpenRouter clients, target probes when needed)

attacker_pod_runtime.base_image: ghcr.io/pi-dev/agent OR pi.dev official image, pinned to a specific tag (ralph must look up the latest tag from pi.dev docs)
attacker_pod_runtime.fallback_base_image: If no official image: ubuntu:24.04 + install PI CLI per pi.dev docs.
attacker_pod_runtime.language_inside_pod: Whatever PI's runtime is (likely TypeScript or a wrapped binary). The Go side never runs inside the pod.

tree_after_M6.README.md: short top-level readme (created in M6, not before)

internal/.pi/: PI agent invocation wrapper (used inside pod binary if any, OR pure helpers)
internal/.openrouter/: thin OpenRouter client (used by control plane for mutation, NOT by pods)
internal/.nimble/: Nimble.ai client — crawl and proxy APIs used by pods to attack the target
internal/.spawn/: high-level pod orchestrator: build manifest, launch, await, parse
internal/.store/: in-memory run state + JSON summary writer
internal/.attacks/: Go-side genome catalog + mutation helpers (data, not Python ports)

pi-attacker/.entrypoint.sh: starts PI agent with TARGET_URL + GENOME envs and prints final JSON line
pi-attacker/.skills/attacker/: PI skill files (yaml/md per pi.dev format). Ralph must research pi.dev docs first.
pi-attacker/.prompts/: system prompt + user prompt templates that reference Nimble

deploy/.rbac.yaml: ServiceAccount + Role (empty perms; pods need none)
deploy/.pod-template.yaml: Reference manifest; the Go code is the source of truth and renders pods programmatically

scripts/.build-and-push-attacker.sh: doctl registry login && docker build && docker push
scripts/.run-local.sh: wrapper that loads .env and invokes the openzerg binary

architecture.summary: Local Go binary `openzerg` reads TARGET_URL, then runs an evolution loop. Each generation: render N pod manifests with random genomes, kubectl-apply via client-go, stream stdout of each pod in parallel, wait for pod exit, parse the final JSON line as the probe result, score fitness, delete pod. Survivors (fitness > 0.1) feed into a mutation step (Go-side mutation + optional OpenRouter call) that produces the next generation's genomes. Loop ends on fitness=1.0 in any pod, OR after MAX_GENERATIONS, OR on user Ctrl-C. Final summary JSON + a short Markdown narrative are written to stdout and to ./out/summary-<run_id>.{json,md}.
architecture.diagram_ascii:
+--------------------+
|  user shell        |
|  TARGET_URL=...    |
|  ./openzerg        |
+---------+----------+
          |
          v
+--------------------+        +------------------------+
|  control plane     |------->|  DigitalOcean K8s      |
|  (Go binary, local)|        |  namespace: openzerg   |
|  - evolve loop     |        |  pods: attacker-g<N>-* |
|  - k8s client-go   |<-------|  one PI agent per pod  |
|  - openrouter MUT  |  logs  |  (Gemma 4 + Nimble)    |
|  - summary writer  |        +-----------+------------+
+---------+----------+                    |
          |                                v
          |                    +------------------------+
          |                    |  TARGET_URL (Juice     |
          |                    |  Shop on Railway)      |
          |                    +------------------------+
          v
  ./out/summary-<id>.json + .md
components: OpenRouter (LLM provider, used by both control-plane MUT step and inside attacker pod)
Nimble.ai (crawl + proxy APIs, used inside attacker pod to attack the target)

components.purpose: Local CLI that orchestrates the evolutionary attack swarm against TARGET_URL and writes the final Outcome summary.
responsibilities: Parse CLI flags + env vars; validate TARGET_URL is set.
Load OPENROUTER_API_KEY and NIMBLE_API_KEY (warn but do not exit if MUT step keys are absent; pods need NIMBLE_API_KEY though).
Connect to k8s cluster via $KUBECONFIG or in-cluster fallback.
For each generation 1..MAX_GENERATIONS: render pod specs from current population, create pods, stream logs, await completion, parse JSON result line, score fitness, save to in-memory store.
Mutate survivors (fitness > 0.1) into next generation. Use OpenRouter (Gemma 4) when key present; fallback to pure-Go random mutation when not.
Stop on first fitness==1.0 OR after MAX_GENERATIONS.
Always delete pods after parsing results (whether success or error).
Print a human-readable narrative to stdout.

subcommands.name: run
subcommands.desc: Run a full evolution. The primary command.

subcommands.name: doctor
subcommands.desc: Print env / k8s / registry / secret status, no side effects.

flags.name: --target
flags.desc: Target URL to attack. Full URL with scheme.

flags.name: --dry-run
flags.desc: Don't actually create pods; emit fake results for plumbing tests.

flags.name: --rate-limit
flags.desc: Aggregate ceiling across all pods. Passed into each pod env.

components.purpose: A disposable container that runs a single PI agent attempt against TARGET_URL with a given GENOME, then prints exactly one final JSON line to stdout.
responsibilities: Configure PI to use OpenRouter with model google/gemma-4-31b-it (preferred) or google/gemma-4-26b-a4b-it (fallback if needed).

Equip PI with the 'attacker' skill (in /home/agent/.pi/skills/attacker/) which has tools for: making HTTP requests, calling Nimble's crawl and proxy APIs to interact with the target, parsing JSON, and reporting findings.
System prompt: white-hat scope, target URL, attack vector hint (from GENOME), rules of engagement, output schema.
On agent completion (or timeout), the entrypoint MUST emit exactly one final line to stdout matching the result schema (see data_contracts.attacker_result_jsonl).
Exit 0 on a clean run regardless of breach/no-breach. Exit 2 on infrastructure error (missing key, network down).
env_vars_received: GENOME (JSON string with vector, technique, params, hint)
components.output_contract: Last non-empty line of stdout MUST be a single-line JSON object matching attacker_result_jsonl schema. All other stdout lines are free-form logs (recommended: structured JSON lines with type=log).

components.purpose: Cluster prerequisites: namespace, ServiceAccount, optional egress NetworkPolicy.
components.secret_creation: Create the openrouter+nimble Secret manually OR via `openzerg doctor --create-secret`. NEVER commit real secret YAML.

pi.what: PI is the agent framework that runs inside each attacker pod. It executes the LLM loop, calls tools (Nimble, HTTP), and reports findings.
pi.docs: https://pi.dev/docs (ralph MUST WebFetch this on first M3 iteration before writing the Dockerfile; record the exact CLI/image name in docs/ARCHITECTURE.md)
pi.local_install_hint: /home/carson/.pi/agent/models.json already exists with OpenRouter+Gemma 4 configured. Mirror that config inside the pod image.
research_questions_for_ralph: What is the exact PI CLI invocation that runs one session non-interactively?
What is PI's skill manifest format (yaml schema)?
How does PI expose tools to the model (functions vs schemas)?
Does PI have an official Docker image? If yes, what tag?
How does PI surface tool errors (so we can route them to fitness=0.0)?

openrouter.what: LLM provider. Hosts Gemma 4 for both (a) inside-pod PI agent inference and (b) optional control-plane mutation step.
openrouter.control_plane_usage: When MUT step needs LLM-driven mutation of survivor genomes, the control plane POSTs to /chat/completions with a short prompt: 'These attacks partially worked: <evidence>. Generate N mutated variants targeting <target>. Return JSON array.' Fallback to pure-Go random mutation when key absent.
openrouter.budget_guardrail: Hard cap of 32 OpenRouter calls per run. Track in a counter; abort MUT step and fall back to random when exceeded.

nimble.what: REQUIRED sponsor tool. Nimble provides two APIs the agent uses to attack the target: (1) Crawl API — retrieves fully rendered page content including JS-heavy SPAs like Juice Shop; (2) Proxy API — routes the agent's attack HTTP requests through Nimble's residential/datacenter proxy network to avoid rate-limiting and IP bans during multi-generation runs.
nimble.docs: https://docs.nimbleway.com/llms.txt (ralph MUST WebFetch this on first M6 iteration before writing the Nimble client)
primary_uses: Crawl API: retrieve the fully-rendered DOM of Juice Shop pages (it is an Angular SPA; raw curl returns a shell with no useful content). Use this to discover routes, tokens, and form structures the agent needs to craft attacks.
Proxy API: route the agent's attack requests (SQLi probes, XSS payloads, BOLA requests) through Nimble's proxy network so repeated probes are not blocked before a breach is confirmed.
Optional CVE recon: use the crawl API to fetch recent CVE pages from NVD or CISA to seed Gen-1 attack hints.
nimble.client_package: internal/nimble (Go package). Exposes CrawlPage and ProxyRequest methods. Even though the live calls happen inside the pod, the control plane uses CrawlPage at startup to optionally fetch CVE seeds.
research_questions_for_ralph: What is the exact Nimble Crawl API endpoint URL and request schema (method, headers, body fields)?
What is the exact Nimble Proxy API endpoint URL and how are proxied requests specified (is it an HTTP CONNECT proxy or a request-wrapping API)?
How does auth work for each API — same NIMBLE_API_KEY Bearer header for both, or different schemes?
What does the response body look like for a successful crawl (field names for rendered HTML, status code, URL)?
Rate limits and quotas for both APIs.
The .env.example contains NIMBLE_API_URL with a hex-looking token — resolve whether this is a second key, a session token, or a legacy artifact to ignore.
How to surface crawl/proxy responses as tool outputs to PI (direct JSON passthrough, or wrap in a structured envelope)?

kubernetes.client: k8s.io/client-go, loaded via clientcmd from $KUBECONFIG (default /home/carson/.kube/config) with in-cluster fallback.
kubernetes.rbac: Not required for control plane (uses user's kubeconfig). Pods need ServiceAccount with NO permissions (default token can be off).
kubernetes.image_pull_secret: Pods pull from DO registry. Either (a) use a docker-registry imagePullSecret created from `doctl registry kubernetes-manifest | kubectl apply -f -` or (b) accept that the cluster is already linked to the registry. Ralph: try (b) first.

control_plane_env.OPENROUTER_API_KEY: string, optional for control plane (only needed for LLM-driven mutation)
control_plane_env.NIMBLE_API_KEY: string, optional for control plane (needed if CVE seeding enabled)

fields.category: string, one of: injection, auth, access_control, xss, data_exposure, validation
fields.hint: string, free-form, seed for the PI prompt

schema.status: string, one of: BREACH | PARTIAL | RECON | NOOP | ERROR
schema.raw_findings: array of objects with {url, method, status_code, snippet}

attacker_result_jsonl.rule: MUST be the final non-empty stdout line. MUST be single-line JSON. Control plane parses by scanning stdout from the end.

schema.breach: object|null with {pod_id, vector, category, evidence, raw_findings}
schema.per_generation: array of {generation, population, survivors, best_fitness, breaches}

final_summary_md.shape: Title, target, outcome line, then a short narrative (5-15 short paragraphs) explaining: what we tried in Gen 1, what survived, what mutations were applied, and the path to the Outcome. Written by control plane; for v1, generated from a Go template (no LLM). Stretch: LLM polish via OpenRouter.

rules_in_priority_order.if: evidence contains any of: 'admin token', 'auth bypass', 'arbitrary file read', 'sql error revealed schema', 'rce'
evidence contains any of: 'reflected payload', 'reflected xss', 'sql syntax error', 'jwt accepted with alg none', 'directory listing'
evidence contains any of: 'endpoint exists', '200 ok', 'version banner', 'robots disclosure', 'package.json exposed'

nimble_test_schema.philosophy: Light but complete coverage of the four failure modes that can sink the demo: (1) missing key, (2) network error, (3) malformed response, (4) accidental key leak. Each test is small (10-30 lines) and uses httptest.NewServer to fake the Nimble endpoint. NO mocks of internal types. NO tests that just re-state what the function does.

required_tests.name: TestCrawlPage_MissingKey
required_tests.purpose: Client constructed without NIMBLE_API_KEY must return a typed ErrMissingKey, NOT panic and NOT make any HTTP request.
required_tests.shape: Construct client with empty key; call CrawlPage; assert errors.Is(err, nimble.ErrMissingKey); assert no HTTP server was hit.

required_tests.name: TestCrawlPage_HappyPath
required_tests.purpose: Given a fake Nimble crawl server returning a valid response, the client parses it into the typed CrawlResult and the response contains the expected fields.
required_tests.shape: httptest.NewServer returns a canned JSON body matching the Nimble Crawl API response shape; call client.CrawlPage; assert rendered_html, status_code, and url fields populated; assert no error.

required_tests.name: TestCrawlPage_NetworkError
required_tests.purpose: When the Nimble crawl endpoint is unreachable, the client returns a wrapped error and does NOT panic.
required_tests.shape: Point client at a closed port (127.0.0.1:1); call CrawlPage with a short context deadline; assert err != nil; assert err contains 'nimble' for traceability.

required_tests.name: TestCrawlPage_MalformedResponse
required_tests.purpose: When Nimble crawl returns 200 with garbage JSON, the client returns a parse error rather than crashing or returning zero-value data silently.
required_tests.shape: httptest.NewServer returns 'not json at all {{{'; call CrawlPage; assert err != nil; assert err wraps a json.SyntaxError or equivalent.

required_tests.name: TestCrawlPage_HTTP5xx
required_tests.purpose: When Nimble crawl returns 500/502/503, the client returns a typed error containing the status code.
required_tests.shape: Table-driven over 500, 502, 503; httptest returns each; assert client returns ErrUpstream and the error stringifies the status code.

required_tests.name: TestProxyRequest_HappyPath
required_tests.purpose: Given a fake Nimble proxy endpoint, ProxyRequest routes the attack request through and returns the proxied response body and status.
required_tests.shape: httptest.NewServer returns a canned proxied response; call client.ProxyRequest with method=GET and a target URL; assert status and body pass through correctly.

required_tests.name: TestKeyNeverLogged
required_tests.purpose: The most critical test. Set NIMBLE_API_KEY to a known sentinel value ('test-sentinel-xyz123'). Run CrawlPage against a fake server that 500s (forcing the error path, which is the most likely place a careless dev logs the key). Assert the sentinel does NOT appear in any log output, error message, or returned struct.
required_tests.shape: Capture slog output to bytes.Buffer via slog.New(slog.NewTextHandler(buf,...)); inject buffer as test logger; force a 500 on CrawlPage; assert !strings.Contains(buf.String(), sentinel); assert !strings.Contains(err.Error(), sentinel). Repeat for ProxyRequest.
required_tests.criticality: HARD REQUIREMENT — this test passing is part of M6 acceptance criteria #3.

non_required_tests_to_skip: Do NOT write a test that just checks 'Client struct is constructed with the right field'.
Do NOT write a test that mocks the Nimble service interface — the package itself IS the mockable seam.
Do NOT test the PI-side tool wrapper from Go. That wrapper is exercised by the M6 manual verify step (pod logs contain 'nimble_crawl' or 'nimble_proxy').

smoke_test_outside_of_go_test.name: scripts/nimble-smoke.sh
smoke_test_outside_of_go_test.purpose: Optional 1-command live-call smoke test for use during demo prep. Reads NIMBLE_API_KEY from .env, calls the real Nimble Crawl API for the Juice Shop URL, prints the rendered HTML length and HTTP status. Exits 0 on success, 1 on any error. Not run by `go test`. Run manually before the demo.
smoke_test_outside_of_go_test.creation: Optional in M6; recommended but not required for acceptance.

test_helpers.fake_server_helper: Define a small newFakeNimbleServer(t, response) helper in client_test.go (NOT a separate testdata package). Returns *httptest.Server. Keep it under 20 lines.
test_helpers.no_global_state: Each test must construct its own client and fake server. No package-level test setup. No t.Parallel() unless needed for speed.

mutation_strategy.primary: Pure-Go: shuffle params, swap technique within category, vary target_path from a curated list, occasionally cross-breed (swap params between two survivors).
mutation_strategy.secondary_if_openrouter_available: Send survivors' evidence+params to Gemma 4 with a prompt asking for N mutated variants. Validate response is a JSON array matching genome_schema; fall back to primary on parse failure.

stop_conditions: any pod returns fitness==1.0 (status==BREACH) -> stop immediately, write summary with outcome=BREACH
generation count == max_generations -> stop, write summary with outcome=EXHAUSTED
context cancelled (Ctrl-C) -> write partial summary with outcome=EXHAUSTED and a note about cancellation

seed_genomes_for_generation_1.hint: Try classic auth bypass via SQL tautology in email field.
Try reflected XSS via search query parameter.
Try fetching admin user by ID without auth.
Forge a JWT with alg=none claiming admin.
Try to reach admin UI directly.
Try stored XSS in feedback comment field.
Classic Juice Shop search SQLi for user table leak.

tasks: Initialize go.mod with module path github.com/TheApexWu/openzerg/backend.
Create backend/cmd/openzerg/main.go that prints 'openzerg vX.Y' and exits 0.
Create empty packages internal/{config,k8s,pi,openrouter,nimble,evolve,spawn,store,attacks,secrets} each with one .go file declaring the package.
Add .editorconfig if helpful.
Update .gitignore to add ./out/, ./bin/, *.exe.
DO NOT delete the Python files yet.
acceptance: go build ./... exits 0.
go vet ./... exits 0.
Binary prints a non-empty version string.

milestones.name: Config, secrets, doctor command
milestones.goal: openzerg doctor reports k8s connectivity, registry config, and secret presence without making any cluster changes.
tasks: Implement internal/secrets: load .env (godotenv), overlay process env, expose typed Config.
Implement internal/config: CLI flags via stdlib flag or cobra, merge with env, produce a RuntimeConfig.

Wire `openzerg doctor` to print: kubeconfig path resolved, current-context, namespace status (does it exist?), registry login probe, OPENROUTER_API_KEY presence (yes/no, never print value), NIMBLE_API_KEY presence.
Add k8s client construction in internal/k8s with both in-cluster and out-of-cluster paths.
Add a no-op `openzerg run --dry-run` that prints what it would do but does nothing.
acceptance: doctor prints a clean, multi-line report.
doctor exits 0 even when keys are missing (it must not crash).
run --dry-run prints a planned-pod-spec preview and exits 0.

milestones.name: K8s pod spawn + log streaming (no PI yet)
milestones.goal: Control plane can create a pod that runs `echo '<final-json>'`, stream its logs, parse the final JSON line, and delete the pod. End-to-end against the DO cluster.
tasks: Create backend/deploy/namespace.yaml and apply via `openzerg run` first-time setup.
Implement internal/spawn: render a Pod object (k8s.io/api/core/v1) with image=busybox:1.36, command=['sh','-c','echo log1; echo log2; sleep 1; echo {"type":"result",...}'].
Implement internal/k8s: CreatePod, StreamLogs (follow=true), WaitForCompletion, DeletePod.
Implement internal/evolve.ParseLastJSONLine.
Glue: openzerg run launches 3 pods in parallel, prints their results, exits.
Add tests for ParseLastJSONLine with hostile inputs (no trailing newline, multiple json lines, garbage).
Do NOT yet integrate fitness scoring or generations.
verify: kubectl -n openzerg get pods (should show pods being created and deleted during a real run)
acceptance: 3 pods are visible in kubectl during the run.
All 3 results are parsed and printed to stdout.
All 3 pods are deleted after the run (no leftovers in kubectl get pods).
Tests for the parser pass.

milestones.name: Attacker pod image with PI + Gemma 4 (no Nimble yet)
milestones.goal: A real registry.digitalocean.com/openzerg/pi-attacker:latest image that, given TARGET_URL and a GENOME, runs a single PI session against the target using Gemma 4 via OpenRouter, and prints exactly one final result JSON line.
tasks: FIRST: WebFetch https://pi.dev/docs (and any sub-pages discovered) to learn exact CLI invocation, image name, and skill format. Record findings in docs/ARCHITECTURE.md.
Write backend/docker/pi-attacker/Dockerfile: base on PI's official image if it exists, else ubuntu:24.04 + PI install per docs.
Write backend/docker/pi-attacker/entrypoint.sh: read env, write PI config, invoke PI non-interactively, capture output, append result JSON line.
Author backend/docker/pi-attacker/skills/attacker/skill.yaml + instructions.md per PI's actual skill spec (NOT a guess).
Author backend/docker/pi-attacker/prompts/system.tmpl with white-hat scope, target URL placeholder, genome placeholder, output schema instructions.
Build and push: scripts/build-and-push-attacker.sh, runs `doctl registry login` then `docker buildx build --platform=linux/amd64 -t registry.digitalocean.com/openzerg/pi-attacker:latest --push .`.
Create k8s Secret openzerg-keys via `kubectl create secret generic openzerg-keys --from-env-file=.env -n openzerg`.
Update internal/spawn to render the real pod spec referencing the new image and the Secret.
Run: openzerg run --population 3 --generations 1 against juice-shop URL. Pods should attack but no fitness scoring yet — just verify the result line shape.
verify: docker run --rm -e TARGET_URL=https://juice-shop-production-d0c5.up.railway.app -e GENOME='{...}' -e OPENROUTER_API_KEY=$KEY registry.digitalocean.com/openzerg/pi-attacker:latest # prints valid JSON last line
kubectl -n openzerg logs <pod> | tail -1 # parses as JSON matching attacker_result_jsonl
acceptance: Image builds and pushes.
Pod runs to completion within activeDeadlineSeconds.
Final stdout line parses as the result schema.
No literal API key appears in image layers or pod env (verify with kubectl describe pod).

milestones.name: Evolution loop, fitness scoring, mutation, summary
milestones.goal: Full N-generation run with survivors, mutation, stop conditions, and the final summary.{json,md} artifacts.
tasks: Implement internal/evolve/fitness.go per data_contracts.fitness_scoring.
Implement internal/evolve/mutate.go: pure-Go mutation (primary).
Implement internal/openrouter client: minimal /chat/completions POST.
Implement internal/evolve/mutate_llm.go: optional LLM mutation via OpenRouter, guarded by feature flag + budget counter.
Implement internal/store: in-memory RunStore tracking per-pod results and per-generation aggregates.
Implement summary writer: ./out/summary-<run_id>.json AND .md. Markdown is a Go text/template.

Wire run loop: Gen1=seeds; for each gen: spawn POPULATION_SIZE pods, await all, score, pick survivors (cap at SURVIVOR_CAP), mutate to fill next pop, repeat. Stop on fitness=1.0 or gen==MAX_GENERATIONS.
Add cancellation: SIGINT writes a partial summary and exits 0.
Add tests for fitness.Score, mutate.Mutate, and ParseLastJSONLine.
acceptance: A full 4-generation run completes in under 12 minutes wall clock.
summary.json validates against final_summary_json schema.
summary.md is human-readable and explicitly names the Outcome (BREACH or EXHAUSTED) in the first 3 lines.
On BREACH, summary.md prints the exact request that worked (path, method, payload, response snippet).
Ctrl-C mid-run still produces a partial summary.

No HTML/Phaser/Replay artifacts remain in repo.

A sample summary artifact is committed.

milestones.name: Nimble integration (sponsor tool — required)
milestones.goal: The PI agent inside each pod can call Nimble to fetch JS-rendered pages from the target, and the control plane optionally uses Nimble at startup for CVE seeding. Nimble must be observably exercised during the demo run so the judging Tool-Use criterion is satisfied. Comes LAST because the rest of the system must already be green so any failure surface is isolated to the Nimble integration.
milestones.rationale: M0-M5 deliver a working end-to-end attack swarm without Nimble (PI alone counts as one sponsor tool but we need a second). M6 layers Nimble on top so we can verify each piece in isolation. If Nimble is partially broken at demo time, M0-M5 still demo cleanly with --disable-nimble.
tasks: Research first: WebFetch https://docs.nimbleway.com/llms.txt and any linked sub-pages. Resolve the questions under integrations.nimble.research_questions_for_ralph. Record findings in docs/ARCHITECTURE.md under a 'Nimble Integration' section.

Implement backend/internal/nimble: Go client with two methods: CrawlPage(ctx, targetURL, opts) for retrieving fully-rendered page content via Nimble's Crawl API, and ProxyRequest(ctx, method, targetURL, body, opts) for routing attack HTTP requests through Nimble's Proxy API. Use NIMBLE_API_KEY from env. Return typed responses, never raw bytes.

Add a thin tool wrapper inside the attacker pod image (backend/docker/pi-attacker/tools/nimble_tools) that PI can invoke as skill tools. Wrapper exposes two tools: nimble_crawl and nimble_proxy. Reads NIMBLE_API_KEY from env, calls the appropriate Nimble API, returns JSON to PI.

Update backend/docker/pi-attacker/skills/attacker/skill.yaml to expose both tools to PI: 'nimble_crawl: Use when you need the fully rendered DOM of a target page (Juice Shop is Angular — curl returns an empty shell). Returns rendered HTML and status code.' and 'nimble_proxy: Use when you want to route an attack request (SQLi probe, XSS payload, BOLA request) through Nimble\'s proxy network to avoid IP-level blocking during repeated probes.'

Update at least 2 seed genomes in PRD.json's evolution_loop.seed_genomes_for_generation_1 to flag them as requiring Nimble (add a 'requires_nimble': true field). Recommended: score_board, admin_section. NOTE: editing PRD.json IS allowed in M6 — this is the one milestone that may modify the PRD.

Add a control-plane startup hook in cmd/openzerg/main.go: when --enable-cve-seed flag is set AND NIMBLE_API_KEY is present, call nimble.CrawlPage(ctx, 'https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/search/results?query=juice+shop') and extract the top CVE snippet to append as a 'hint' on one Gen-1 genome. Default for the flag is FALSE for demo determinism; we enable it manually only if we want CVE-flavored demo footage.

Add a --disable-nimble flag to openzerg run for the failure-mode demo case (so the rest of the swarm still works if Nimble has a bad day at demo time). When set, the pod env does not include NIMBLE_API_KEY and the skill tool returns a stubbed 'nimble disabled' error to PI.
Write the Nimble test suite per data_contracts.nimble_test_schema (see below). Light but covers all four failure modes: missing key, network error, malformed response, key leak.
verify: go test ./internal/nimble/...   # all schema tests green
go test ./...                   # full project still green
./bin/openzerg doctor           # reports 'NIMBLE_API_KEY: present' and 'nimble: reachable' (or 'demo' if no key)
./bin/openzerg run --target https://juice-shop-production-d0c5.up.railway.app --population 5 --generations 1   # at least one pod's log contains 'nimble_crawl' or 'nimble_proxy' tool call
./bin/openzerg run --target ... --disable-nimble --population 3 --generations 1   # completes cleanly with no nimble_crawl or nimble_proxy calls (proves the kill-switch works)
acceptance: At least one nimble_crawl or nimble_proxy tool call is observably logged per generation when Nimble is enabled.
Nimble failures are non-fatal: pod still emits a result with fitness >= 0 and the run continues.
Nimble client NEVER leaks NIMBLE_API_KEY into pod logs, control-plane logs, or summary artifacts. Verified by the key-leak test.
--disable-nimble produces a clean run with zero nimble_crawl or nimble_proxy calls (verified by absence of both tool names in pod logs).
docs/ARCHITECTURE.md has a 'Nimble Integration' section documenting the resolved Crawl API and Proxy API endpoint shapes.
summary.md from a Nimble-enabled run mentions which probes used nimble_crawl or nimble_proxy in their attack path (or notes 'no Nimble usage' if zero).

demo_must_show: kubectl -n openzerg get pods --watch in a second pane showing pods spawning per generation
Live stdout narrative: Gen 1 results, survivors named, Gen 2 spawns, eventually a BREACH or a 'best_fitness=X.X EXHAUSTED' message
Final summary.md opened in editor, showing the path the swarm took to its Outcome
code_must_satisfy: go build ./... is green at every commit on main.
No real API keys in git history.
No goroutine leaks: every spawned pod has a matching delete in a defer.
Every external call (OpenRouter, Nimble, k8s) is wrapped with a context.Context and a timeout.

iteration_protocol: 2. Identify the lowest-numbered milestone that is not yet ACCEPTED in STATE.md.
3. Identify the smallest next task within that milestone.
4. Make the smallest possible code change that advances that task.
5. Run the milestone's verify commands. If anything is red, fix forward; do not move on.
6. When all of a milestone's acceptance items pass, update STATE.md to mark it ACCEPTED and write a one-line summary.
7. git add -A && git commit -m '<conventional-commit-msg>' (no co-author trailer).
ralph_loop.start_command: cd /home/carson/openzerg && (test -f backend/go.mod && cd backend && go build ./... || echo 'M0 not done')
ralph_loop.stop_condition: All milestones M0-M6 marked ACCEPTED in STATE.md AND `go build ./...` is green AND a sample ./out/summary-*.json exists with outcome in {BREACH, EXHAUSTED}.

example_subjects: feat(spawn): create and delete pods via client-go
fix(evolve): handle missing trailing newline in pod logs

never_do: Never commit any value of OPENROUTER_API_KEY or NIMBLE_API_KEY.
Never attack anything other than TARGET_URL.
Never skip a milestone's verify step.
Never add Datadog code in v1 (it's a deferred stretch goal).
Never push the control-plane image to the registry (it runs locally).
Never delete the kube-system namespace or DigitalOcean-managed system workloads (do-*, csi-*, cilium*, konnectivity-*).
Never run `kubectl delete ns openzerg` mid-run; only create. (Cleanup of the openzerg namespace at the start of a clean rebuild is fine.)
must_research_before_writing_code: Before M3: WebFetch https://pi.dev/docs and resolve all 'research_questions_for_ralph' under integrations.pi.
Before M6: WebFetch https://docs.nimbleway.com/llms.txt and resolve all 'research_questions_for_ralph' under integrations.nimble.
escape_hatches: If PI has no usable Docker image, build from base ubuntu:24.04 and install PI CLI via its documented installer.
If Nimble's API shape doesn't match assumptions, encode whatever IS in their docs and update the PRD via STATE.md (do NOT edit PRD.json silently; record deviations).
If Gemma 4 is too slow or rate-limited via OpenRouter, the 26b variant is available as fallback; if still bad, document and proceed with whatever works.
If the DO image registry can't be pulled from the cluster, create an imagePullSecret via `doctl registry kubernetes-manifest` and add it to the pod spec.
